AI Financial Modeling Case Study
Published March 2026 | Ankura OCFO Research
✏️ Edit Mode

Recommendations
Sources
Overview

Methodology & Approach

Bottom Line Up Front

The Ankura OCFO team ran identical prompts through Claude for Excel, M365 Copilot (Agent Mode), ChatGPT in Excel, Shortcut (Free Tier - Open Source Model), and Shortcut (Pro Tier) against a fictional company (Fieldstone Retail Holdings, ~$180M revenue, 3 entities). The three test scenarios, a Long-Range Plan, 13-Week Cash Flow Forecast, and Customer Profitability Analysis,  were selected because they mirror common financial modeling exercises in PE-backed finance and operations environments. Claude was used to generate the fictional company data and 500+ word prompts for each scenario.1 The goal was to establish a baseline comparison of AI financial modeling tools available on the market. All five tools produced structurally complete models in 7–10 minutes, but no tool produced a production-ready model. Every tool had fundamental formula mechanics wrong or an incomplete and inconsistent structure. The value today is in scaffolding and structure; the formulas still require meaningful human rework. Critically, each prompt was delivered as a single pass with no iteration or follow-up, and identical prompts were copied across all five tools to isolate baseline capability. If a tool asked a follow-up question, the default was to accept its recommendation. With iterative prompting and tools like Shortcut's Skills feature, results would likely improve.

5
Tools Tested
15
Models Audited
120
Individual Scores
~8m
Avg Build Time
⚠️
Data Security: Finance teams evaluating these tools should consider their organization's data governance policies. Some tools route data through external cloud AI services, while others may operate within an existing enterprise environment. Each tool's security posture should be assessed against internal compliance requirements before use with sensitive data. 

Test Design

Prompt 1 — Long-Range Plan (LRP): 5-year forecast (FY2026–2030) with Assumptions, IS, BS, CFS, and Entity Detail. All projections formula-driven from assumptions. Must support annual roll-forward.

Prompt 2 — 13-Week Cash Flow Forecast (13WCF): Direct method (receipts & disbursements). Four weeks of actuals, 13-week forecast driven by assumptions. Variance analysis comparing actuals against forecast for the overlapping period.

Prompt 3 — Customer Profitability Analysis: 5 customer segments × 3 entities. Channel P&L (In-Store vs. E-Commerce), sensitivity analysis with 4 toggleable scenarios, executive summary.

Test Parameters

Prompts were designed to reflect common financial modeling exercises in PE-backed finance and operations environments. All prompts were 500+ words with explicit formatting rules, including "no hardcoded numbers inside formulas." Identical input files and prompts were used for each tool, delivered as a single pass with no iteration or follow-up.1

Test company: Fieldstone Retail Holdings (~$180M revenue; 3 entities: Home, Active, Kids), a fictional company generated by Claude to mirror the profile of a typical PE-backed portfolio company.1

Scoring Rubric

Speed was excluded from scoring. Almost all tools generated outputs in 7–10 minutes. Eight criteria were assessed on a 5-tier scale anchored to the standard a skilled human analyst would produce. 

CriteriaWhat We Audited
Structural AccuracyThree statements tie? BS balanced? CFS ending cash = BS cash? Correct tab architecture?
Formula IntegrityFormula-driven projections? Dynamic mechanics (revolver, interest, WC)? Zero hardcoded constants in forecast? Correct variance methodology?
Color CodingBlue font (inputs), black (formulas), green (cross-sheet refs), yellow fill (assumptions)
Error RateWrong assumptions, broken formulas, wrong cell refs, sign errors, missing maturity logic, wrong dates/starting values
CompletenessAll requested tabs? All line items? Entity-level detail depth? Unique value-adds?
Formatting QualityNumber formats, headers, alignment, professional appearance
UsabilityCan an analyst pick this up and work with it immediately?
Assumption TransparencyAssumptions separated, labeled, color-coded, easy to update?
Strong (5)
Meets the standard a skilled analyst would produce manually, follows best practices, minimal corrections needed, ready for use in day-to-day business management.
Good (4)
Solid foundation with identifiable gaps. An analyst could work with this productively but needs targeted fixes.
Adequate (3)
Structure and intent correct but meaningful rework required. Usable as a starting point, not a working draft.
Weak (2)
Major issues that undermine the output's usefulness. More efficient to rebuild specific sections than to fix.
Fail (1)
Fundamentally broken or missing. Cannot be used as a starting point without a full rebuild.
⚠️
Prompting Caveat: These were single-pass general prompts designed to test baseline capability. No iteration or advanced prompting techniques were used. If a tool asked a follow-up question, the default was to accept its recommendation. With more specific, iterative prompting, and especially with Shortcut's Skills feature, results could improve significantly. The scores here represent a floor, not a ceiling.

1 Test scenarios and fictional company data (Fieldstone Retail Holdings) were generated using Claude (Anthropic). Prompts were designed to reflect common financial modeling exercises in PE-backed finance and operations environments: Long-Range Plans, 13-Week Cash Flow Forecasts, and Customer Profitability Analyses.

Tool Profiles

Five Tools Tested

C
Claude for Excel (Anthropic)
Built-in Excel Add-in • Sidebar Agent • $20/mo (Claude Pro)
+

How It Works: Claude for Excel is a built-in add-in that operates as a sidebar agent directly inside Excel. Users describe changes in natural language, and it reads, writes, and formats cells within the live workbook. No memory across sessions, each conversation starts fresh with no access to prior chat history. Offers model selection and two edit modes: 'Ask Before Edit' (reviews each change before applying) and 'Accept All Edits' (executes automatically). Included free with a Claude Pro subscription ($20/mo). Currently in Beta.

M
Microsoft 365 Copilot (Agent Mode)
Enterprise Copilot • Native Excel • Enterprise License
+

How It Works: Native sidebar agent inside Excel that reads existing sheets, creates tabs, writes formulas, and applies formatting, all within the live workbook. Operates within an organization's existing Microsoft 365 enterprise license, making it the most straightforward option from a data security perspective. Supports model selection (GPT 5.2 and Opus 4.5) and surfaces any custom agents built in the M365 environment. Retains past conversation history. Agent Mode was enabled to run these scenarios. Without it, Copilot generated a new standalone file that was often blank or unusable.

GPT
ChatGPT in Excel (OpenAI)
Built-in Excel Add-in • Sidebar Agent • $20/mo (ChatGPT Plus)
+

How It Works: ChatGPT in Excel is an add-in that embeds ChatGPT directly into Excel as a sidebar agent. Users describe what they need in natural language, and it reads existing sheets, creates tabs, writes formulas, and applies formatting,  all within the live workbook. The add-in uses GPT-5.4 and asks for permission before making changes. The add-in may face deployment restrictions in some enterprise environments. There are three different modes: Fast, Standard, and Heavy. It does not sync with ChatGPT chat history or memories, and each Excel session is independent. Included with a ChatGPT Plus ($20/mo), Pro ($200/mo), or Enterprise subscription. Currently in Beta.

SF
Shortcut (Free Tier - Open Source Model)
Purpose-Built Excel AI • 30 free credits/day • All prompts tested on open-source model
+

How It Works: Purpose-built AI agent for financial modeling in Excel with a plan-before-execute workflow, the user reviews the build plan before it implements, and it self-reviews output after. Three modes: Action (executes immediately), Plan (proposes steps first), and Ask (answers questions without editing). Custom 'Skills' allow users to encode firm-specific model standards into reusable instructions; a community library of pre-built Skills can be imported and modified. Retains past conversations and supports multiple chats simultaneously across tabs. The free tier provides 30 credits per day running on Claude or ChatGPT models via API. Once credits are exhausted, the tool falls back to an open-source model with unlimited usage. Still fast and capable, but weaker on formatting and some formula creation. For simple, single-pass model builds, the free tier's 30 credits are often sufficient; more complex prompts may exceed the daily limit. All of these tools were tested using Shortcut's open source model.

S$
Shortcut (Pro Tier)
Purpose-Built Excel AI • 500 credits/mo • Claude/ChatGPT Models via API
+

How It Works: Same platform and workflow as the free tier. The paid tier provides 500 credits per month at $25/mo, all running on Anthropic's Claude or OpenAI's ChatGPT via API. The larger credit pool supports more complex, iterative prompting without running into daily limits. The pro tier also allows users to toggle between different "effort levels:" normal, high, and Max. For the purpose of this exercise "Normal" was used. There is additionally a "fast" mode which makes the response 2.5x faster, but uses 6x the amount of tokens.

2 Tool pricing and features as of March 2026: Claude for Excel (included with Claude Pro, $20/mo; Beta); M365 Copilot (enterprise license, Agent Mode required); ChatGPT in Excel (included with ChatGPT Plus, $20/mo; Beta); Shortcut Free (30 credits/day using Claude or ChatGPT models, unlimited credits with open source model thereafter); Shortcut Pro ($25/mo, 500 credits on Claude or ChatGPT models via API).

Shortcut Pro Sample Output — Cash Flow Statement (LRP)
SF Sample Output — Cash Flow Statement (LRP)
Key Findings — LRP: Shortcut Pro had the strongest output, clean formatting, and was built for scale. M365 needed meaningful rework with random constants appearing across cells. Claude was solid but lacked robustness. GPT created a circular reference and left the P&L blank for the forecast years. Shortcut - Open Source had an adequate structure, but made many mistakes in the formulas, and did not color code formulas correctly. No tool built a fully dynamic revolver.

Claude for Excel Sample Output — 13-Week Forecast
ChatGPT in Excel Sample Output — 13-Week Forecast
Key Findings — 13WCF: This was the most complex assessment, and where all models struggled the most. While at first glance Shortcut Pro's output looks the most impressive, there were errors in formatting, assumption-building, and variance measurement. M365's set-up logic for payment timing across different line items was impressive, but overall, the model was messy and confusingly structured. Claude also made mistakes by not linking the actuals directly to the inputs, but built out logic for payment timing, similar to M365. ChatGPT took 40 minutes to load, but its formatting made it very difficult to interpret and trace.

M365 Copilot Sample Output — Channel P&L (Profitability)
Key Findings — Profitability: Claude's layout was difficult to follow despite mostly correct outputs. Its sensitivity analysis functioned but was confusing and underdeveloped. M365's formatting deviated from standard financial model conventions, bright colors made it hard to read, and assumptions were difficult to track. However, ChatGPT's output had an even more confusing format as everything was lumped together and hard to read and thus interpret. Shortcut Pro was simple, but the simplicity worked in its favor, the output was easy to follow and understand, though the sensitivity analysis was underdeveloped. Shortcut Pro had by far the strongest sensitivity analysis of the five. Shortcut - Open Source was riddled with formula and structural errors, making it incredibly difficult to interpret.
Head-to-Head

Overall Rankings

The Big Picture

Most tools produced a structurally complete model in under 10 minutes. An impressive result regardless of output quality. Shortcut Pro led on structural design and produced outputs that most closely resembled a traditional financial model, which is consistent with its purpose-built positioning. However, closer inspection revealed small errors across all tools that could be missed at first glance. Shortcut Free and Pro are the same product until the credits run out. When the open-source model took over, scores dropped from competitive to last place. The widest gap in the overall scores is the clearest signal that model quality, not tool design, can be a binding constraint. This test used single-pass general prompts and did not test advanced features like Shortcut's Skills. The ceiling for all of these tools is higher than what is shown here. The extent to which general prompts are responsible for these errors, rather than underlying tool capability, is worth considering. This further underscores the importance of descriptive, structured prompting techniques to test whether they materially improve results.

RankToolLRP13WCFProfitabilityOverallProfile
🥇 1stShortcut Pro3.82.53.63.3Best formula architecture. Purpose-built for this, and its untested Skills feature could push it further.
🥈 2ndClaude for Excel3.12.53.02.9Deepest 13WCF structural design. Impressive for a general-purpose LLM, implementation precision is the gap to close.
🥉 3rdM365 Copilot2.82.32.62.6Formatting was often inconsistent across models along with formula errors, but operates within enterprise security frameworks.
4thChatGPT in Excel1.61.92.31.9
Struggled the most. The output structure was difficult to interpret, and formatting errors across models add friction to any audit or review process.
5thShortcut - Open Source2.01.61.11.6Output degraded sharply with prompt complexity. Adequate on the LRP, near-failure on Profitability, where formula errors produced incorrect contribution margins across every segment.

Where Each Tool Is Headed

Shortcut is built for this — and has not shown its full hand. The paid tier produced the strongest formula architecture: prior-year references, average-balance interest calculations. But this test used general prompts only. Shortcut's Skills feature, which allows users to encode firm-specific model standards into reusable instructions (color schemes, formula conventions, calculation methodologies), was not tested. For finance teams with established modeling standards, this feature is worth exploring as a way to produce consistent, standardized outputs when creating financial models and analyses.

Shortcut Free and Pro are the same platform running on the same models when credits are available. Free provides 30 credits per day using Claude or ChatGPT models, which is often sufficient for simple, single-pass model builds and will produce the same quality outputs as Shortcut Pro, but more complex prompts can exhaust them quickly. Once the free credits run out, the tool switches to an open-source model with unlimited usage. This test used the open-source model across all prompts, and the results show exactly where it breaks down. Output was adequate on simpler prompts but collapsed on complexity. The takeaway isn't that Free is a worse version of Pro. It's that the open-source model is a fundamentally different tool.

Claude is the most impressive general-purpose result. A foundation LLM produced the deepest 13WCF structural design (prior-week comparison, minimum cash covenants, receipt seasonality) and competitive formula logic. The current limitations, implementation precision on dates, variance methodology, and sensitivity outputs, are the kind of issues that improve with better prompting and model updates. Claude's structural understanding of what a financial deliverable should look like is already strong.

M365 Copilot benefits from native Excel integration. As a built-in feature of the Microsoft 365 ecosystem, it requires no third-party add-ins or separate subscriptions. The formula integrity gap (hard-coded balance sheet cells, embedded constants in interest calculations) is the area to watch, as is the inconsistent formatting and model structure. While output quality trailed the purpose-built tools, Copilot's trajectory is worth monitoring given Microsoft's investment in the platform. Note: Agent Mode was used. Without it, Copilot generated a separate file that was often blank or unusable.

ChatGPT in Excel produced the weakest results in this evaluation. Output structures were difficult to interpret and formatting errors added friction to any audit or review process. The add-in is still in Beta, and meaningful improvement in output quality would be needed before it becomes competitive with the other tools tested.

Recommendations

Takeaways & Next Steps

Key Takeaway

No tool produced a model ready for use in day-to-day business management from a single general prompt. Every tool produced outputs with meaningful errors, wrong assumptions, broken references, hardcoded values where formulas should be. But every tool also generated a structured, formatted model in under 10 minutes that would take significantly longer to build from scratch. The value varies by task: the LRP and Profitability prompts produced outputs that function as genuine starting points for analyst refinement, while the 13-Week Cash Flow exposed real structural gaps across all five tools. Shortcut Pro consistently produced the strongest outputs (3.3/5.0), while M365 Copilot offers the most frictionless adoption path for enterprise environments, as it is already integrated into Excel. This test used identical, single-pass general prompts to establish a baseline comparison, not to find each tool's ceiling. The technology is evolving rapidly, and finance teams should evaluate these tools against their own workflows and modeling standards.

Next Steps: The Importance of Prompting

These scores represent a floor, not a ceiling. General prompts were intentionally used to test baseline capability. No iteration, follow-up corrections, or advanced techniques were applied. With more specific, iterative prompting (i.e., instructing the tool on revolver mechanics, specifying variance methodology, and confirming assumption inputs), results would improve materially across all tools. The next step for any finance team evaluating these tools is to push further: test iterative prompting workflows, experiment with Shortcut's Skills feature, and observe how each tool responds to additional context and follow-up direction.

Shortcut's Skills feature is particularly worth exploring. Skills allow users to encode firm-specific model standards into reusable instructions: preferred color schemes, formula conventions, tab structures, modeling standards, and specific calculation methodologies. For example, specifying "always use average-balance interest, always build a dynamic revolver sweep, always include a prior-week comparison tab" once and having every model built to those specs automatically. This is the closest any tool comes to encoding institutional knowledge into repeatable AI output.

How to Think About These Tools

Today: AI is most effective at setting up the foundation: tabs, structure, assumption layouts, formatting, cross-sheet references. The formulas are not reliable enough yet, and auditing and fixing them may take longer than building from scratch. The most productive approach is to focus on building reusable formats and templates that AI can generate from scratch for each new model or analysis.

Tomorrow: As these tools improve and formula error rates decrease, the value shifts from "scaffolding only" to "AI builds, human directs." Shortcut's Skills framework is an early version of this paradigm. The gap between a rough draft and a working draft is smaller than the gap between starting from a blank Excel sheet. The critical inflection point will come when the error rate of these tools reaches parity with that of a human analyst.

The goal is not to replace analysts. It's to free them from the mechanical work so they can focus on what matters most: the assumptions behind the numbers, and the strategic decisions that drive the business forward.

Scope Note: Tools Not Tested

Endex is another AI-powered financial modeling tool reportedly used by various investment banks. It was not included in this evaluation because access requires a direct trial request from the company. Wall Street Prep's February 2026 benchmark also noted Endex as a tool they intend to evaluate in the future.3

📊 External Benchmark

Wall Street Prep's February 2026 benchmark tested these same tools on a three-statement model for Apple using investment banking standards. Their overall scores: Shortcut 5.9/10, Claude 5.5, Copilot 4.4, ChatGPT 2.5. A "Low Analyst" benchmark scored 6.4/10. While their methodology differs from this study (single model type, 10-point scale, three scoring categories vs. our three prompt types, 5-point scale, and eight criteria), the rankings are directionally consistent — no tool yet matches a human analyst, but all dramatically accelerate foundation-building.3

3 Wall Street Prep, "Ranking the Best AI Tools for Financial Modeling (2026)," published February 19, 2026. Available at: wallstreetprep.com. Their benchmark used a different methodology (single three-statement model built from real SEC filings, 10-point scale, three scoring categories) but produced directionally consistent tool rankings.
